# Transcrição: 1. Define a Contact

- **Fonte:** 1. Define a Contact
- **Duração:** 03:21
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:01]

So preparing for the call. Hey, what is a contact? People tell me all the time, oh, I have a contact. What is that? What is a contact? We're preparing for a cold call now. You're going to be taking a lot of call volume, contacts, appointments, presentations, or demonstrations. The first thing we need to do for you to be effective on a phone, to be able to measure whether you're effective, is to define what a contact really is. You can't just count a call as a contact. I mean, I guess you could. Oh, I called them. I left a message. You need to define what a true contact is. Here are a few things that I would consider. Your organization needs to make these decisions, but these are the few things I would consider necessary to call it a contact. Number one, I spoke with a decision maker. Now a decision maker does not mean that they are the check signer, but they are a decision maker. That would be one indicator. Number two, I left a voicemail with a decision maker. That would be considered a contact. Number three, I spoke with the gatekeeper, not the decision maker. I would consider that a contact. Number four, I spoke with an influencer in the organization, not the gatekeeper, not the decision maker. Number five, I left a voicemail with an influencer at the mid-level management level, not the decision maker, not the gatekeeper, not even a check writer or a decision maker, not somebody that sat on the board, but they influenced the decision. Look, if you call and don't leave a message, that ain't a contact. I called. I made contact. No, you didn't leave a message. You always, always, always leave a message, always. Okay. Now, I know some of you disagree with this because you're like, if I leave too many messages, they're not going to call me back. If you don't leave a message, they're not going to call you back. Always leave a message. Always, always, always. I'll tell you one other trick. When I leave a message, hey, Bob, Grant Cardone, give me a call. Here's my number. I'm going to hit zero. It goes to voicemail. I'll leave a message on voicemail and then I hit zero to go back to the receptionist so that I can talk to a second person. Maybe I get an influencer now. Maybe I get a gatekeeper. See, the perfect contact would be this. Three contacts at the same company. That I would count as one contact. See, I'm not, I'm not trying to make my numbers look good. I'm trying to feed my kids. I'm trying to feed my dreams. I'm trying to take care of my spouse, my wife, right? I'm going to leave a message with a middle management, the decision maker, an influencer, the gatekeeper, another manager. I might go all the way down to somebody that just got hired there. I might leave 12 messages with 12 different people. In order to be considered a contact, look, it's got to be able to be measured, okay? It's got to be quantifiable. It's got to be not just in your books, but you left the message with them. It's got to be in the prospect's memory. Some place they can find it. You're not trying to make your numbers look good. You're trying to make contact, right? Hey, anybody home? Do you know I even made contact? Do you know about me?

## [03:01 - 03:21]

Did I leave a message? Did I make an impact? Did I set a hook? Did I create some interest? Did I at least bother someone? Great. You want to bother people? Look, I want to make myself known, okay? I got to get out of obscurity. Follow-up is about making a contact. Your definition of contact is making contact, not just on your books, but also on the prospect's books.
